coredata.dc:description: The use of graphene quantum dots as biomedical devices and drug delivery systems has been increasing. The nano-platform of pure carbon has shown unique properties and is approved to be safe for human use. In this study, we successfully produced and characterized folic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQD-FA) to evaluate their antiviral activity against Zika virus (ZIKV) infection in vitro, and for radiolabeling with the alpha-particle emitting radionuclide radium-223. The in vitro results exhibited the low cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe GQD-FA in Vero E6 cells and the antiviral effect against replication of the ZIKV infection. In addition, our findings demonstrated that functionalization with folic acid doesn’t improve the antiviral effect of graphene quantum dots against ZIVK replication in vitro. On the other hand, the radiolabeled nanoprobe 223Ra@GQD-FA was also produced as confirmed by the Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy analysis. 223Ra@GQD-FA might expand the application of alpha targeted therapy using radium-223 in folate receptor-overexpressing tumors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the synthesis of GQD-FA via a carbodiimide functionalization reaction.
Characterization of GQD-FA by FTIR. The FTIR spectra showed the OH, CC, aromatic CC, amide CO, and CN stretching vibrations in the sample of GQD-FA (A; in red).
Characterization of GQD-FA by PXRD. The PXRD pattern showed the fingerprint bands of folic acid (B; in black) in the sample of GQD-FA (B; in red).
The morphologic analysis by AFM. (A) The height map of pure GQD and (B) its 3D view. (C) The height map of GQD-FA and (D) its respective 3D-view. Blue arrows highlight QDs.
C 1s (A) and O 1s (B) XPS patterns of GQD-FA by XPS.

EDS results from the analysis in scanning electron microscope of the nanoprobe 223Ra@GQD-FA after two days (A; 150×, 5 kV, 7.3 mm) and fourteen days (B; 200×, 15 kV, 7.2 mm) of radiolabeling. The upper panel from both A and B shows the EDS mapping of elemental carbon (yellow), nitrogen (turquoise), radium (purple), and lead (pink) in the 223Ra@GQD-FA sample. The bottom panel from both A and B represents the EDS mapping of these elements altogether, the EDS spectra, and the elemental quantification expressed as weight and atomic percent.

Cytotoxic effect of nanoparticles loaded with GQD or GQD-FA. Vero E6 cells were cultured for 48 h in MEM medium in the presence or absence of different concentrations of GQD, GQD-FA, and Triton X100 as a positive control for cell death. After this period, the supernatant was collected, and the cytotoxicity was evaluated by LDH quantification.

Effects of GQD and GQD-FA loaded nanosystems on ZIKV in vitro infection of Vero E6 cells. Cells were treated for 1 h with GQD (light gray) or GQD-FA (dark gray) with different concentrations as indicated in the x-axis or maintained with culture medium only (Untreated − 0). After, cells were infected with ZIKV in a MOI of 0.05 and incubated for 2 h. Then, infected cells were washed and treated again as indicated or remained untreated. After 48 h, viral RNA in culture supernatants was determined by RT-qPCR using specific primers and probes against the ZIKV Env gene. Data are mean ± SEM of two independent experiments. Data were analyzed by two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparison test, where *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.005.
Representative size of GQD-FA in comparison with pure GQD.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of graphene quantum dots as biomedical devices and drug delivery systems has been increasing. The nano-platform of pure carbon has shown unique properties and is approved to be safe for human use. In this study, we successfully produced and characterized folic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQD-FA) to evaluate their antiviral activity against Zika virus (ZIKV) infection in vitro, and for radiolabeling with the alpha-particle emitting radionuclide radium-223. The in vitro results exhibited the low cytotoxicity of the nanoprobe GQD-FA in Vero E6 cells and the antiviral effect against replication of the ZIKV infection. In addition, our findings demonstrated that functionalization with folic acid doesn’t improve the antiviral effect of graphene quantum dots against ZIVK replication in vitro. On the other hand, the radiolabeled nanoprobe 223Ra@GQD-FA was also produced as confirmed by the Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy analysis. 223Ra@GQD-FA might expand the application of alpha targeted therapy using radium-223 in folate receptor-overexpressing tumors.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of carbon-based nanomaterials as biomedical smart materials is attracting attention, mostly due to the possibility of forming several compounds with diverse geometries and chemical properties [1–4]. Among the carbon-based nanomaterials, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) shows several properties, which include i) distinctive and tunablephotoluminescence [5], ii) solubility in water [6], iii) photostability [7], iv) goodbiocompatibility [8,9] and v) low toxicity and cytotoxicity [10,11].

Although the several properties of the GQDs, their use in the medical/biomedical field is still restricted due to the lack of targeting. This, otherwise, can be overdue by functionalizing the GQDs with proteins [12], polymers [13], biomolecules, and others [14]. The functionalization of GQDs’ surface beside the increment in targeting can also improve biodegradability, safety, and aqueous solubility [15]. In addition, the functionalization process improves delivery efficiency through reduced clearance and prolonged retention in the body [16]. The functionalization may be triggered by a great sort of interactions, which include: covalent, ionic, Van der Waal or π–π, grafting the functional groups over the surface of GQDs [17–19].

In previous works, GQDs have been functionalized with folic acid (GQD-FA) for cancer cell imaging and optical detection of Hg2+ [20] as well as for ratiometric pH sensing and specific multicolor imaging in living cells [21]. Folic acid (FA), or folate, represented chemically as C19H19N7O6, is composed of a heterocyclic pterin structure [22,23]. It’s an essential vitamin participating in the biosynthesis of nucleotides, amino acids, neurotransmitters, and certain vitamins [24–26]. Recently, FA has been shown to be effective against ZIKV infection by inhibiting its replication, being associated with the folate receptor-α (FRα)-AMPK signal transduction pathway [27]. ZIKV is a mosquito-borne flavivirus [28]. However, the antiviral effect of GQD-FA against Zika virus (ZIKV) infection is not stated yet, to the best of our knowledge.

In addition, the coupling of GQD-FA with radionuclides for targeted alpha therapy (TAT) in nuclear medicine is another interesting application not assessed yet to the best of our knowledge. Folic acid binds to folate receptors with high affinity, which are overexpressed in many cancers such as breast cancer cells [29,30]. The presence of π–π stacking interactions as Van der Waal and covalent interactions increase the stability of chemisorption and physisorption of radionuclides on the surface of these compounds [31–33]. In effect, our group successfully radiolabeled non-functionalized GQDs with radium-223 [34], and technetium-99 m [35] for other goals.

TAT is an appealing alternative for the treatment of patients with multiple micrometastases [36]. To date, radium-223 dichloride ([223Ra]RaCl2) is the single alpha particle-emitting radiopharmaceutical specifically approved for the treatment of bone metastases in the clinic [37]. The physical properties of radium-223 include a half-life [t1/2] of 11.4 days, with alpha-particle emitting energy of 27.4 MeV with<0.1 mm of penetration [34]. The development of 223Ra-based radiopharmaceuticals is increasing nowadays; however, their use is quite limited due to their self-targeting, mainly to bone tissues mimicking the calcium metabolism [34,38].

Therefore, in this study, GQD-FA was synthesized, characterized, and in vitro evaluated against ZIKV infection. The radiolabeling of QGD-FA with radium-223 was also reported as the first step to be performed in the development of the radiolabeled nanoprobe to further expand the application of TAT beyond bone cancer.

GQDs (Graphene Quantum Dots) were previously produced and described [35]. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Oakwood Chemical and Thermo Scientific, respectively. Folic acid and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Proquímios (Brazil) and ACS Cientifica (Brazil), respectively. The [223Ra]RaCl2 (Xofigo ®) was purchased from Bayer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots decoration process with folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid decorated graphene quantum dots (GQD-FA) was prepared via a carbodiimide functionalization reaction, following previous reports from our lab [39]. Briefly, GQDs were dispersed in water (74 mL) by stirring at room temperature for ten minutes. Then, a solution of EDC (112 mg) and sulfo-NHS (297 mg) in distilled water (18.5 mL) was added to the GQDs dispersion. Subsequently, the mixture was stirred for 4 h at room temperature. Next, a solution of folic acid (88 mg) in DMSO (9 mL) was added to the mixture and stirred for 18 h at room temperature in the dark. The resulting material was centrifuged (13 500 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with distilled water to remove unbound reagents. The quality of the reaction was confirmed by Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), atomic force microscopy (AFM), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

Infrared absorption spectra in an IR-tracer-100 Shimadzu equipment were captured using the method FTIR with UATR accessory (universal sampling arm accessory with pressure) and were reached at room temperature on a spectrophotometer with transformed by Fourier from Perkin Elmer (model: Spectrum 400, Serial No: 82287) in the region between 4000 and 500 cm−1. The spectral resolution was 2 cm−1 and 32 accumulations.

To estimate the characteristics of GQD-FA, PXRD measurements were performed using a Bruker AXS D8 Advance diffractometer, equipped with the LynxEye linear detector and with a CuKα radiation source, operating at 40 mA/40 kV. PXRD measurements were carried on the 2θ = 15–68° range with 0.02 step size and a counting time of 0.2 s/step.

Samples of GQD-FA were solubilized in Milli-q water, and 10 µm was dropped in fresh cleaved mica and rested until complete drying. The AFM measurements of graphene QDs film were performed using a Multimode 8 microscope (Bruker, Santa Barbara, CA) in QNM (Quantitative Nanomechanics) mode using probes model Scanasyst Air, with nominal spring constant of 0.4 N/m and nominal tip radio of 2 nm approximately. The structural parameters of the pure GQD and GQD-FA were calculated using the Gwyddion 2.57 software, applying the boundary grain detection to the topographic images (500 nm scan area), and masking the regions corresponding to the QDs. From these regions, statistical information on the height and diameter of pure GQDs and GQDs-FA was calculated.

Chemical surface analyses on GQD-FA powder were performed by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using the SPECS PHOIBOS 100/150 spectrometer with a hemispheric analyzer operating at 1486.6 eV of Al Kα. The spectra were collected with a high-resolution polychromatic X-ray source within an energy step of 0.02 eV. A processing software (CasaXPS, SPECS Software) was used to adjust the peak positions of O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s levels and determine the chemical binding energies (BEs) of some species formed, as well as to calculate the relative atomic quantities on the samples. The spectra were calibrated by using the hydrocarbon at C 1s = 284.6 eV.

A solution of EDC (1.5 mg) in Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 9.4; 1 mL) was added to the dispersion of GQD-FA (7.5 mg) in distilled water (2.5 mL). Then, an aliquot of [223Ra]RaCl2 (49.3 kBq; 1 mL) was added. The mixture was heated at 90 °C for 1 h under stirring. Finally, the reaction mixture was dried by heating at 150 °C, and stored in a desiccator. The obtention of the nanoprobe 223Ra@GQD-FA was confirmed by Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS) in a scanning electron microscope.

The EDS imaging and elemental analysis of 223Ra@GQD-FA were recorded by a Bruker XFlash MIN SVE detector and Scan Generator coupled with a Hitachi TM3000 Table Top Scanning Electron Microscope (Japan), using the QUANTAX 70 EDS system. The parameters used for the analysis were: magnification of 150× and 200×; sampling time of 120 s; low vacuum with a high-sensitivity semiconductor backscattered electron detector; accelerating voltage of 5 kV and 15 kV; a working distance of 7.2 mm and 7.3 mm; and spot size (area) of 324 × 226 µm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of GQD and GQD-FA in Vero E6 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Vero E6 cells (ATCC, #1586) were cultured in MEM medium supplemented with 1 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco) and plated at 100,000 cells/well in a 48-well plate. The cells were incubated in the presence of different concentrations (7.5, 15, and 30 µg/mL) of GQD, GQD-FA, or with 1 % Triton X-100 as a positive control for cell death, at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 48 h. After this period, 100 µL of the supernatant was collected and transferred to a 96-well plate. Then, to quantify lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) activity, 100 μL of reagent mixture from the “Cytotoxic Detection Kit (LDH)” Kit (Roche) was added per well and incubated for 30 min at room temperature in the dark. After this period, the reaction was stopped with the addition of 50 μL of 1 N HCl, and the absorbance was read at 492 nm in an EnSpire Multimode Plate Reader (PerkinElmer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Zika viral load determination
ce:section.ce:para: Vero E6 cells (ATCC, no 1586) were cultured in Minimum Eagle’s medium (MEM; Gibco) supplemented with 10 % FBS (Gibco) and plated at 100,000 cells/well in a 48 well plate at 37 °C with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Twenty-four hours later, the supernatant was harvested and the cells were pre-treated for 1 h with 100 μL of medium containing different concentrations of the GQD and GQD-FA. After this period, the ZIKV IEC isolate (3rd passage) at a MOI of 0.05 was added to allow infection for 2 h. Then, the supernatants were removed, and the cells were washed twice with PBS 1x and cultured in the presence of fresh medium for further 48 h at 37 °C with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The virus yield in culture supernatants was quantified by RT-qPCR using specific primers and probes against the Env gene [40]. Briefly, total RNA was isolated using QIAamp® Viral RNA kit (Qiagen) and the reaction was performed using AgPath ID kit (Thermo Fisher) in a 7500 Real-Time PCR System (Applied Biosystems). A standard curve was generated using synthetic RNA, and after determining the number of viral RNA copies in each sample the graphs were generated using the GraphPad Prism 7 software.

The results were expressed as the mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM). The statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 7 software (San Diego, CA, USA). Data were analyzed by two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparison test, where *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.005.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots decoration process with folic acid

ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2) of GQD-FA showed a broad absorption band, centered at 3435 cm−1, related to O-H's stretching vibration as it was observed for the pure GQDs [39]. The other broad absorption band, centered at 1628 cm−1, in the GQD-FA sample is ascribable to both the aromatic CC skeletal ring vibrations from the GQD domain (1621–1656 cm−1) [41,42] and the stretching vibration of CO from the formed amide bond (1640–1664 cm−1) [20,39,43,44]. The additional weak bands of GQD-FA at 2922 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching [45], while the weak absorption at ∼ 1194 cm−1 is related to the CN stretching [46].

The PXRD spectra of GQD-FA showed several sharp lines, indicating the crystallinity of the sample. The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 5.4, 10.9, 13.1, and 16.4°matched with the PXRD pattern of pure folic acid, evidencing the presence of the folic acid in the GQD. The PXRD pattern of pure folic acid agrees with the literature [47] with slight shifting.

The AFM images exhibited pure GQD (Fig. 4A) and GQD-FA (Fig. 4C) and three-dimensional views (Fig. 4B and D, respectively). Moreover, the diameter of the GQD-FA and GQD samples are 25.4 ± 2.7 nm and 13.6 ± 3.1 nm, respectively. The maximum heights observed over both GQDs, pure and folic (pointed by the blue arrows), are of the order of pN, demonstrating the few atomic layers present in these structures. These results emphasize the coupling of graphene quantum dots with folic acid, corroborating the FTIR and PXRD data.

Fig. 5 shows the XPS results of the sample GQD-FA. In C 1s peak (Fig. 5A), binding energies (BE) around 284.7, 286.0, and 289.2 eV were observed, which represent the bond type of CC/CHx [48] (produced for QGD and folic acid), CN/COH [49,50] and OCO [48] (contribution of folic acid), respectively. On the analyzed surface, the contribution of bound folic acid was 49.9 at%, with respect to CN/COH and OCO bond types. In the O 1s peak (Fig. 5B), the main contribution was of the (CO) [20] type around 533.8 eV. The other O 1s peak at 532.2 eV could be assigned to the COH bond type [21,51]. In addition, a slight contribution of metallic oxide [48] was also observed in these samples, due to contamination in the prepared material. This is owing to the presence of the Al 2p peak observed in the survey spectrum (not analyzed in high-energy resolution measurements). The peak of N 1s showed a BE at 400.2 eV, which represents the NHCO bond type of folic acid [50].

The EDS results confirmed the radiolabeling of GQD-FA with radium-223. The analysis of the 223Ra@GQD-FA sample after two and fourteen days of radiolabeling demonstrated the elemental presence of radium and lead (Fig. 6 ). The presence of lead is associated with the formation of lead-211 (t1/2 = 36.1 m) and lead-207 (stable) as products of radium-223 decay. Consequently, the weight and atomic percent of lead increased over time as expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of GQD and GQD-FA in Vero E6 cells
ce:section.ce:para: VeroE6 cells were cultured in the presence or absence of different concentrations of GQD, GQD-FA as well as in the presence of Triton X100 as a positive control for cell death. After 48 h, the culture supernatant was collected, and an evaluation of the cytotoxic effect was performed by quantification of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). The cytotoxicity detection kit (LDH) is a method that directly quantifies cell death in culture. The assay is based on the measurement of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) released into the culture medium when cell membrane integrity is lost. Fig. 7 shows the percentage of viable cells in cultures treated with GQD, GQD-FA, or Triton. GQD displayed a significant decrease in cell viability just at the highest concentration tested (30 µg/ml). In contrast, the different concentrations tested of GQD-FA induced a value of <1 % of the cytotoxic effect. These results allow us to conclude that GQD-FA does not have considerable toxic effects on cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Zika viral load determination
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential antiviral effect of GQD or GQD-AF, we performed in vitro ZIKV infection in Vero E6 cells pre-treated with these nanosystems and evaluated RNA copy numbers in culture supernatants 48 h post-infection. The treatment with both nanosystems reduced ZIKV RNA copy numbers in a dose-dependent manner, compared to the untreated control group (Fig. 8 ). However, no significant difference was found in the viral inhibition between GQD and GQD-AF -treated groups. These results demonstrate that both nanosystems were able to inhibit ZIVK replication in vitro.

The synthesis of GQD-FA has been previously reported by other groups using diverse protocols, delivering products with different average particle sizes, determined by Transmission Electron Microscopy. Li, R. et al. (2018) prepared GQD-FA of 5.2 nm via thermal pyrolysis at 180 °C of maleic acid as a carbon source for making graphene sheets, and folic acid [20]. On the other hand, Hai, X. et al. (2018) prepared GQD-FA of 33.6 nm via a one-pot microwaved alkali cutting method, using graphene oxide and folic acid as raw materials [21]. In the present work, we directly coupled folic acid with GQDs by a crosslink carbodiimide reaction, delivering a product with 25.4 nm.

The energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy demonstrated the attachment of [223Ra]Ra+2 in the GQD-FA forming the 223Ra@GQD-FA. According to Sanchez-Cano [58], energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy has great potential to investigate the nano-bio interface. Also, energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy is widely used for the determination of radioisotopes in samples [59,60].

Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of GQD-FA was assessed and compared with pure GQD in Vero E6 cells. The results demonstrated the safety of GQD-FA, which is in concordance with the literature that used HeLa cells [20,21]. GQD-FA exhibited a significantly lower cytotoxic effect (<1 %) compared with pure GQD. The significant decrease in the cell viability with the increase of the concentration of pure GQD from 15 to 30 µg/mL may be due to the GQD aggregation into a larger cluster (by the increased concentration), forming pores in the lipid membrane [61]. In previous works, however, pure GQD did not present a significant cytotoxic effect in human myeloma cells and ovarian cancer cells [39]. The cytotoxicity degree of GQD also depends on surface modification. Xie et al. [62], demonstrated that OH-GQD was toxic while COOH-GQD and NH2-GQD were non-cytotoxic within the measured concentration. In addition, OH-GQD, COOH-GQD and NH2-GQD activated or inhibited the autophagy pathways in a varied manner. Furthermore, these authors stated that autophagy played a protective role against the toxicity of GQD [62].

Finally, both GQD and GQD-FA demonstrated their in vitro antiviral effect against replication of the ZIKV infection. Other works also exposed the antiviral activity of GQDs against the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) [63]. Nevertheless, GQDs have been mostly evaluated as biosensors for the detection of hepatitis B and hepatitis E viruses [64,65], HIV [66], as well as mosquito-borne viruses such as ZIKV, dengue virus, and chikungunya virus [67]. Our findings corroborated the antiviral properties of carbon nanomaterials, as stated in the literature [68], and also demonstrated that functionalization of GQDs with folic acid doesn’t improve their antiviral effect against ZIVK replication in vitro, statistically.

The nanoprobe GQD-FA was easily produced by a crosslink carbodiimide reaction and exhibited a significantly low cytotoxic effect in VeroE6 cells. Furthermore, both GQD-FA and pure GQD inhibited ZIVK replication in vitro without significant differences, statistically. On the other hand, GQD-FA was successfully radiolabeled with radium-223, which is very attractive to further expand the application of targeted alpha therapy in cancer using 223Ra-labeled nanoparticles.
